pi_items.metadata_value: Ammonia (most consistent toxicity, most frequent violations)

pi_items.source: Violation counts calculated from actual data in setup_task.sh

pi_items.verified_value: 0.25 ppm is standard aquarium test detection threshold; 0 ppm is ideal but ammonia >0.25 indicates poor biofiltration
pi_items.source: Aquarium Science, AquaInfo, and pet aquarium guides - 0.25 ppm is the API test standard threshold for indicating biofiltration issues

pi_items.verified_value: Nitrite should be <0.2 ppm ideally, but 0.5 ppm is commonly used as an action threshold
pi_items.source: Aqueon, Aquarium Science, and Fritz Aquatics - nitrite reduces fish oxygen-carrying ability; 0.5 ppm is recognized action threshold

pi_items.verified_value: Ideal <20 ppm, safely up to 40 ppm, above 50 ppm undesirable
pi_items.source: Aqueon, FDACS, NT Labs - 40 ppm is middle of acceptable range; >50 ppm is concerning

pi_items.verified_value: pH 6.5-7.5 is standard recommendation for community freshwater aquariums
pi_items.source: Aqueon, FDACS, Glass Grown Aquatics - pH affects fish breathing, digestion, and reproduction

privileged_info_summary: The aquarium water analysis task uses 14 days of synthetic water chemistry data (2024-01-01 to 2024-01-14) with measured violations: Ammonia >0.25ppm on 13 days (avg 0.3786ppm), Nitrite >0.5ppm on 9 days (avg 0.5486ppm), Nitrate >40ppm on 12 days (avg 47.0ppm), and pH stable at 0 violations (avg 7.1429, range 6.9-7.4). The primary water quality problem is Ammonia due to most frequent violations. The thresholds used (Ammonia >0.25ppm, Nitrite >0.5ppm, Nitrate >40ppm, pH 6.5-7.5) align with established freshwater aquarium standards from sources like Aqueon and Aquarium Science. However, the setup script incorrectly documents expected violation counts: claims 11 Ammonia violations (actual 13), 7 Nitrite violations (actual 9), and 13 Nitrate violations (actual 12).
